graphql_batching.description: GraphQL batch query abuse — send arrays of queries in a single request to bypass rate limiting, brute-force OTPs, or enumerate data without triggering per-request throttles

probes.description: Send 5 batched queries — if all return results, batch queries are accepted and rate limits can be bypassed by packing N operations into 1 HTTP request
Batched login mutations — 3 credential attempts in a single request bypassing per-request rate limiting on authentication

probes.payload: {"query":"query { alias1: user(id:1){email} alias2: user(id:2){email} alias3: user(id:3){email} alias4: user(id:4){email} alias5: user(id:5){email} }"}
probes.description: Query aliasing to enumerate records in a single request — bypasses rate limits that count HTTP requests but not query complexity

rest_parameter_pollution.description: REST parameter pollution — duplicate parameters, array wrapping, and type juggling to bypass validation or cause unexpected behavior in backend parameter parsing

probes.description: Duplicate parameter — PHP uses last value, ASP.NET joins with comma, Java uses first. Auth middleware may check one occurrence while business logic uses another
Array wrapping — send parameter as JSON array instead of scalar. Backend may iterate and process all values, bypassing single-value validation
Type juggling — send boolean true instead of integer/string. Weakly-typed backends may coerce true to 1, bypassing string-based validation
NoSQL operator injection via JSON object — MongoDB accepts query operators in JSON; $gt with empty string matches all documents
Null byte injection in JSON string — may truncate value at null byte in C-based parsers while JSON parser accepts full string

api_rate_limit_bypass.description: Rate limit bypass via header spoofing, IP rotation indicators, and origin obfuscation — test whether rate limits are tied to easily-spoofable identifiers

probes.description: X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1 — rate limit may key on this header instead of real client IP
X-Real-IP: 10.0.0.1 — alternative IP header that some rate limiters trust for client identification
X-Originating-IP: 192.168.1.1 — less common IP override header, some WAFs and load balancers honor it
X-Client-IP with rotating value — if rate limit resets with different header value, limit is header-keyed
Case variation in path — /api/Login vs /api/login may have separate rate limit buckets

broken_pagination.description: Pagination abuse — negative offsets, extreme limits, cursor manipulation to extract unauthorized data or cause DoS

probes.description: Negative offset/page — may wrap to end of dataset, expose records from the tail, or cause integer underflow
Extreme limit value — API may return entire dataset if max limit is not enforced server-side
Zero limit — some ORMs treat limit=0 as no limit, returning all records
Non-numeric cursor manipulation — inject invalid cursor to trigger error messages revealing pagination implementation (Relay, Keyset, etc.)
Base64-encoded cursor injection {"id":1} — if cursor is a base64 JSON object, manipulating the ID may access arbitrary pages/records

http2_smuggling.description: HTTP/2 request smuggling — H2.CL and H2.TE desync attacks exploiting HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 downgrade in reverse proxies

probes.payload:
POST / HTTP/2
Host: {{host}}
Content-Length: 0
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
probes.description: H2.CL probe — HTTP/2 front-end strips Transfer-Encoding but passes Content-Length; backend receives both headers after H2-to-H1 translation causing desync

probes.payload:
POST / HTTP/2
Host: {{host}}
Content-Length: 4
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

0
probes.description: H2.TE detection — front-end uses content-length from H2 frame, backend parses chunked encoding in downgraded H1 request. Timeout indicates desync.

probes.payload:
POST / HTTP/2
Host: {{host}}
transfer-encoding: chunked
Content-Length: 6

0

G
probes.description: H2 header case smuggling — HTTP/2 requires lowercase headers; proxy may pass mixed-case to H1 backend where Transfer-Encoding is case-insensitive, causing differential parsing
H2 TE with null byte — HTTP/2 binary framing allows null bytes in header values; H1 backend may truncate at null or parse differently causing desync
